summary:
The presence of a clearly defined parasagittal hematoma on CT confirms an organic cerebral pathology due to hypertension, making hypertensive intracranial hemorrhage the most accurate diagnosis over eclampsia—which typically presents with non-structural or diffuse neurologic features. Seizure occurrence in early postpartum women with prior mild hypertension can occur either through eclampsia or hypotensive-related stroke; however, specific findings like parenchymal bleeds are diagnostic indicators favoring vascular causes such as HICH instead of primary maternal encephalic disorders related to preeclamptic syndrome.

The correct diagnosis should reflect both clinical timing and imaging data—the combination pointing directly at hypertensive intracranial hemorrhage being consistent with known pathophysiological mechanisms linking high blood pressure to spontaneous ruptured vessels near major venous sinuses where these hematomas commonly form.

Key point: Imaging findings override purely clinical criteria when evaluating suspected obstetric emergencies because they provide objective proof of tissue damage resulting from elevated pressures within vasculature—a hallmark sign distinguishing true ischemic/hemorrhagic events from other types of seizures associated with metabolic imbalances or preexisting conditions affecting central nervous system function.

Therefore, final decision remains firmly aligned with choice B — Hypertensioin-induced intracranial hemorrhage – which fits perfectly across all domains including onset pattern, risk factors linked to gestational illness progression, symptom profile observed clinically, and crucially, confirmed visualization of actual bleeding site using modern diagnostics tools available today.

In conclusion: Despite initial suspicion towards eclampsia owing to recent childbirth and existing milder elevation in BP levels before labor, real-time assessment reveals no support for assuming absence of proteinuria equates automatically to existence of full-blown eclepsy—it fails here since there exists concrete visual confirmation indicating active bleeding inside skull cavity attributable solely to increased arterial tension acting upon fragile walls adjacent to sagittal sinus region common sites affected under uncontrolled HTN states experienced frequently among multiparous patients exposed repeatedly throughout pregnancies. Hence only one plausible interpretation emerges—that this case represents classic example illustrating how critical role played by neuroradiological evaluation becomes essential whenever any unexpected convulsion occurs immediately after vaginal deliveries regardless whether previous lab tests showed normal results regarding urinary proteins content level found routinely monitored during antenatal visits.

So ultimately we must rely less heavily just on historical signs suggestive enough for presumptive diagnoses—and turn increasingly toward physical examination outcomes combined particularly those
